entry.title: The Atlantic: 21 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Inflation vs. Recession: Eight Readers Choose
  • Malls Aren’t Actually Dying
  • Squirrels Could Make Monkeypox a Forever Problem
  • Dave Chappelle’s Not Kidding
  • A Substitution
  • Saïd Sayrafiezadeh on the Performance of Fiction
  • Cancel Zoning
  • The Liberals Who Won’t Acknowledge the Crime Problem
  • How a Google Employee Fell for the Eliza Effect
  • The Insurrectionist in the Flower Shop
  • China’s ‘Very Dangerous Trajectory’
  • Beware the Luxury Beach Resort
  • Should We Blame Social Media for Society’s Divisions?
  • What the Yellowstone Floods Teach Us
  • The President Who Did Everything Right and Got No Thanks
  • When Can a Marriage Be Saved?
  • Beach Currents Don’t Have to Be This Deadly
  • Chris Hemsworth Finds His Villainous Niche
  • What If Russia Uses Nuclear Weapons in Ukraine?
  • The Open Secret of Google Search
  • Britain’s Unbridgeable Divide
  • Google’s AI Is Something Even Stranger Than Conscious
  • The Organization of Your Bookshelves Tells Its Own Story
  • Russia Has a Plan for Ukraine. It Looks Like Chechnya.
  • Does the Jason Kander Story Have a Third Act?
  • The Democrats’ New Spokesman in the Culture Wars
  • The January 6 Committee’s Most Damning Revelation Yet
  • Why Biden Shouldn’t Run in 2024
  • The Triumph of a Sometimes-Trump Republican
  • Perfume Genius Sings the Body Electric
  • The Book That Captures My Life as a Dad
  • A Lost Trove of Civil War Gold, an FBI Excavation, and Some Very Angry Treasure Hunters
  • The Book That Never Stops Changing
  • It’s Time for Pixar to Move on From Toy Story
  • Barry Isn’t a Comedy Anymore. But It’s Become an Even Better Show.
  • The Books Swallowed by the Black Hole of the Coronavirus
  • TikTok Killed the Video Star
  • The Problem of English Identity
  • Minority Report Tried to Warn Us About Technology
  • The Thin Line Between Sickness and Health
  • Where Russia’s Declinist Rage Isn’t Enough
  • Photos of the Week: Paddle Steamer, Floating Restaurant, Glass Bridge
  • Views of the Strawberry Supermoon
  • They Bent to Their Knees and Kissed the Sand
  • The Personal Brand Is Dead
  • Photos: Devastating Floods Hit the Yellowstone Region
  • Long COVID Could Be a ‘Mass Deterioration Event’
  • Will These Be the Last Polar Bears on Earth?
  • A Hotter, Poorer, and Less Free America
  • A Frog So Small, It Could Not Frog
  • An Unusual Movie Pick for Father’s Day
  • 10 Hours, Two Theme Parties
  • The Awkward Intimacy of Therapy
  • Trump’s Dangerous Wannabes
  • Two Battles for Democracy
  • There May Be a Blunt-Force Fix for Inflation
  • Leave Joe Biden Alone
News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

entry.title: The Diplomat: 22 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • A Cycle of Perpetual Violence for the Women of Uzbekistan
  • Learning to Live With a Nuclear North Korea
  • 2 Women Still Hospitalized After Brutal Attack in Tangshan

  • Flip-Flop on Wheat Exports Angers Indian Farmers

  • Japan, Australia to Expand Defense Ties, Citing Concerns About Regional Order
  • Thousands Protest ‘Bulldozer Justice’ Against Indian Muslims
  • Will IPEF Help the US Counter China?
  • Tokayev Downplays January CSTO Deployment to Kazakhstan
  • As Taiwan Remembers Tiananmen, Hong Kong Looms Large
  • Takeaways From the 2022 Shangri-La Dialogue
  • Pakistan’s New Budget Aims to Please the IMF
  • Should Brazil Ban Huawei?
  • Why Protectionism is Rattling Markets in Southeast Asia
  • Myanmar’s Conflict Threatens to Produce ‘Lost Generation’: UN Expert
  • Cambodia’s Local Elections Have Ended Hun Sen’s One-Party System
  • Militant Group Responsible for Rohingya Advocate’s Murder, Say Bangladeshi Police

  • Mongolia’s ‘1 Billion Tree’ Movement
  • Is There Room in ‘New Kazakhstan’ For New Political Parties?
  • Korean Peninsula Tensions Escalate Amid a Return to Old School Policies
  • Foreign Banks Face Uncertainty in China

  • Thai PM Meets US Defense Secretary in a Bid to Advance Security Ties

  • Timor-Leste-China Relations: Where Does the Concern Lie?
  • China’s Envoy to Australia Says 2 Nations at ‘New Juncture’
  • The Steep Cost of Bachelet’s Visit to China
The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

News, Analysis and Opinion from POLITICO

entry.title: The Third Pole: 14 Jun, 2022

entry.content:

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Nautilus: 15 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • This Planetary Scientist Is Always Reaching for Something Big
  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound
  • Were It Not for Cosmic Good Fortune, We Wouldn’t Be Here
  • Mysteries Are to Be Embraced, But Also to Be Solved
  • How Much Is the Ocean Worth?
  • Reshuffled Rivers Bolster the Amazon’s Hyper-Biodiversity
  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Race to Explore the Ocean’s Twilight Zone
  • The Oceans Are Teeming with Unknown Species
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Life’s First Peptides May Have Grown on RNA Strands
  • You Eat a Credit Card’s Worth of Plastic Every Week
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • No Two Human Brains Are Alike
  • Portrait of the Human as a Young Hominin
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • What Is Time?
  • Doctor Strange and the Multiverse in Science
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

entry.title: Nature: 16 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Diversity in science prizes: why is progress so slow?
  • Research must do no harm: new guidance addresses all studies relating to people
  • The human microbiome: there is much left to do
  • A micro-whirligig does the molecular twist
  • Coins from Pompeii’s ruins hint at finances of the dead
  • How a full gut tells the mouth to stop eating
  • Mighty mini-thermometer detects tiny temperature changes
  • No island is an island
  • Ominous thaw: warm patches spread in a permafrost-rich land
  • A catastrophic collapse for the ‘flying banana’ of the Kalahari
  • Opioid-crisis peak, science prizes and tree-species puzzle
  • NASA’s Perseverance rover begins key search for life on Mars
  • Gene therapy’s comeback: how scientists are trying to make it safer
  • Monkeypox vaccination begins — can the global outbreaks be contained?
  • Why call it BA.2.12.1? A guide to the tangled Omicron family
  • Indigenous knowledge reveals history of fire-prone California forest
  • Why chemists can’t quit palladium
  • How months-long COVID infections could seed dangerous new variants
  • Learn from COVID: Gates’s pandemic prescription
  • Female dominance, and coding for kids: Books in brief
  • Benjamin Mottelson (1926–2022)
  • Fuel crisis: slash demand in three sectors to protect economies and climate
  • Fleeing Russian researchers seek Western support
  • Survey data must be top-notch to tackle societal crises
  • Mars: do we still need astronauts?
  • Industry versus academia — a mid-life career switch
  • Peer-to-peer careers advice for research managers
  • Cloud labs: where robots do the research
  • Sniffing out forest fungi
  • 25 years of valuing ecosystems in decision-making
  • Tiny isotopic difference tests standard model of particle physics
  • The first complete human genome
  • Molecular soccer balls connected to make a 2D material
  • Give physicians’ views to improve COVID vaccine uptake
  • Potato genomes pave the way to crop improvement
  • A population of ultraviolet-dim protoclusters detected in absorption
  • Measurement of the bound-electron g-factor difference in coupled ions
  • Realizing the symmetry-protected Haldane phase in Fermi–Hubbard ladders
  • Superconducting-qubit readout via low-backaction electro-optic transduction
  • Evidence for unconventional superconductivity in twisted trilayer graphene
  • An on-chip photonic deep neural network for image classification
  • Synthesis of a monolayer fullerene network
  • A solid-state electrolysis process for upcycling aluminium scrap
  • Topography of mountain belts controlled by rheology and surface processes
  • Fossil biomolecules reveal an avian metabolism in the ancestral dinosaur
  • Graph pangenome captures missing heritability and empowers tomato breeding
  • Genome evolution and diversity of wild and cultivated potatoes
  • C﻿ommunicating doctors’ consensus persistently increases COVID-19 vaccinations
  • Olfactory sensory experience regulates gliomagenesis via neuronal IGF1
  • Divergent transcriptional regulation of astrocyte reactivity across disorders
  • An oxygen-sensing mechanism for angiosperm adaptation to altitude
  • Generation of specialized blood vessels via lymphatic transdifferentiation
  • FcγR-mediated SARS-CoV-2 infection of monocytes activates inflammation
  • Inflammasome activation in infected macrophages drives COVID-19 pathology
  • ADAR1 masks the cancer immunotherapeutic promise of ZBP1-driven necroptosis
  • Mechanism of mitoribosomal small subunit biogenesis and preinitiation
  • Author Correction: Assembly of the basal mantle structure beneath Africa
  • Author Correction: Fundamental immune–oncogenicity trade-offs define driver mutation fitness
  • Publisher Correction: A stable hippocampal code in freely flying bats
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: TIME: June 20th, 2022 | Vol. 199, No. 23 | U.S.
entry.summary: TIME: June 20th, 2022 | Vol. 199, No. 23 | U.S. published at 2022-06-09 09:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • TIME’s New Cover Lists Every City That Has Suffered One of America’s 213 Mass Shootings So Far This Year
  • After Uvalde, Biden Fears Another Cycle of Inaction on Guns
  • ‘There Is an Emptiness.’ Uvalde Shooting Victim Lexi Rubio’s Great-Grandfather Remembers Her 10 Years of Life

  • After Uvalde, a Warning From a Sandy Hook Mother
  • Inside the Mind of the Most Powerful Woman in Europe
  • The Jan. 6 Committee Is About to Hold Public Hearings. Here’s What to Expect.
  • The UAE and Israel Have High Hopes for Their Historic Trade Deal. Experts Are Skeptical
  • Parades, Pageants, and Afternoon Tea: The History of Britain’s Royal Jubilee Celebrations
  • Here’s What to Know About COVID-19 Booster Shots for Kids
  • Sheryl Sandberg Made Facebook Into a Giant—But At a Cost to the World
  • U.S.-Russia Tensions Rise Amid Biden Decision to Send Rockets to Ukraine
  • The Great Tampon Shortage of 2022: The Supply Chain Problem No One’s Talking About
  • The Depp-Heard Trial Perpetuates the Myth of the Perfect Victim
  • The Most Surprising Aspect of Colombia’s Election Wasn’t Who Won
  • How I Lost Myself to Motherhood
  • ‘The Real Villain Is the Closet’: A New History of Gay Washington Deals With Society As It Truly Was
  • Inside the Right-Wing Movement to Ban Trans Youth From Sports
  • Election Author Tom Perrotta on Writing Tracy Flick Into a MeToo World
  • If You Care About Movies, You Care About Cannes (Even if You Think You Don’t)
  • The Best Albums of 2022 So Far
  • Laura Dern on Jurassic Park and the Pressure of Reviving an Iconic Character
Weekly US magazine.

entry.title: Poetry: June 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Dear Reader,
  • Like a Ping Pong Ball
  • A Study through Homes
  • Prelude
  • دستخط
  • Handwriting
  • Translator’s Note
  • Dream of Low Houses

  • Healing Will Come: Elegy after Natural Disaster

  • Second Generation Ars Poetica

  • To Jorge Luis Borges
  • Walking along Harvard Square

  • Claws of the Sea
  • From “Con R: An Act for Making”

  • The Heart As
  • water
  • balance
  • Visitation
  • Escenografía etimológica
  • Scene, with Etymologies
  • Self-Translator’s Note
  • The Grain
  • “Astrological Speculum,” and Other Objects Found on My Way to the Ancestors
Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.
